Flour · Water · Salt · Time

The art of
wild ferment

From a single sourdough starter named Maya, a humble obsession was born. I bake, share, and write — chasing the quiet beauty of an open crumb.

Meet Maya

About me

What began as a way
to quiet my mind

I joined my first sourdough workshop during an emotionally charged period of my life, searching for something grounding to hold onto. That day, I came home with a bubbling jar of starter named Maya — and somewhere between flour, water, and fermentation, my mind became quiet again.

As I navigated personal changes and new identities, sourdough gave me focus. I watched dough rise at midnight, set alarms for 2am folds, obsessed over crumb structure, and found comfort in the slow rhythm of the process.

I've often heard that sourdough is equal parts science, art, and patience. But over time, I've realised it is also deeply emotional — a reflection of care, presence, frustration, joy, and state of mind. Some loaves fight you. Others surprise you. Some feel almost like healing.

I am still learning every day, and perhaps that is part of why I love it so much. Through this space, I want to share that process honestly: the beautiful bakes, the imperfect ones, casual gatherings, notes from the kitchen, and occasionally, a poem when a particular loaf moves me to words.

— Anjulee

Learn with me

Hands-on workshops in my kitchen

Small groups, flour-dusted aprons, and loaves you take home warm. Each class is intimate — never more than 6 people — so you actually learn.

Workshop

The First Loaf

A hands-on introduction to sourdough, from scratch. We mix dough together, talk through fermentation and folding, and you leave with a loaf of your own — warm, proud, and ready to do it again at home.

🍞 Funny Poetry

"The Sourdough Saga"

My heart broke the week I fed my first starter.
Flour. Water. Tears. Therapy.
It bubbled, I didn't.

That jar became my emotional support organism.
It rose when I couldn't.
It had mood swings,
demanded attention,
and smelled a bit like regret.

Still, it was loyal.
It never left me on read.

Then, plot twist! He came back.
The man, not the starter.
And suddenly, I'm busy "being okay."

Breakfasts replaced by sleep-ins,
texts instead of feedings.
And somewhere between his "I missed you"
and my "maybe we're fine now,"
the starter died.

Didn't even go dramatic.
Just… stopped breathing.

Now he's calm in my life:
steady, sweet, occasionally underbaked.
And the starter?
Maybe it understood the assignment:
to keep me going
until I could start rising for real.

May 2026 · 3 min read
